title: Wood That Waits
passage:
The Baltic keeps its shipwrecks the way few seas do. Its water is cold, nearly fresh, and low in oxygen, and the shipworm that reduces a sunk hull to lace within a decade in warmer seas cannot breed there at all. So the seabed off the Swedish and Estonian coasts still holds wooden ships that went down three and four centuries ago with their masts standing and their carvings crisp. When one such wreck — a merchantman the survey team labelled the Sabina, lying upright in ninety metres of water off Gotland — was mapped in detail two winters ago, the first argument was not about how it had sunk. It was about what to do with it: haul the timbers up into the light, or leave them where the dark had kept them safe.

Some answers came without disturbing the wreck at all. A dendrochronologist at the Kalvik maritime unit, Tobias Renlund, took thin cores from a dozen exposed frames and matched their ring patterns against a master chronology built from Baltic oak. The wood, he found, had been felled in a narrow window around the winter of 1627, and its ring signature pointed not to Gotland but to forests on the southern shore, hundreds of miles from where the ship had come to rest. That alone unsettled the local story that the Sabina had been built and sailed as a Gotland vessel. Renlund is careful about how far the dating reaches. The outer, datable sapwood survived on only a handful of the cored timbers, so the felling year holds firmly for the main hull planking; for the lighter timbers, which may have come from later repairs, he will say only that the evidence is "consistent, not settled".

The deeper question — raise or leave — the team chose to test rather than argue. Twenty loose frames that had already worked free of the wreck were divided in two. Half were brought to the surface and put through the long conservation bath that waterlogged oak needs, soaked for years in polyethylene glycol to replace the water in the cells before the wood is allowed to dry. The other half were reburied in the cold mud beside the wreck and left under instruments that logged their condition season after season. For the first few years the comparison seemed almost cruelly one-sided. The raised, treated frames were clean, catalogued, and on their way to a display case; the reburied ones were invisible, muddy, and doing nothing anyone could photograph. Read only that far, and leaving good timber in the dark looked like a failure of nerve.

Then the longer record came in, and it ran the other way. As the raised timbers dried, they began to do what the salvaged wood of older museum ships had done before them: sulphur compounds soaked up during centuries on the seabed reacted with oxygen and moisture in the gallery air, and a faint bloom of acid crept through the grain, splitting some frames along the ring lines and forcing conservators back for repeated, costly treatment. The reburied frames, sealed again in cold and airless mud, simply held. Season after season the instruments reported almost no change; the very conditions that had preserved the ship for four hundred years went on preserving it. What the museum had gained in access it was slowly paying for in decay, while the timber it had left behind sat unspoiled and unseen.

The unit's senior conservator, Marit Oldenburg, draws a hard line from all this. "Every plank we lift, we begin to lose," she told me. "The sea is the only conservator that never sends a bill. Leave them down there — always, every wreck, without exception — and let the water go on doing what no laboratory can." The record from the Sabina is more divided than her rule allows. It does not show that raising a wreck is a mistake; it shows that raising one is a bargain, in which the price is a slow chemical unmaking that the seabed spares you. A ship left in the mud is a ship no visitor will ever stand before and no student will ever measure by hand. Whether that trade is worth making depends on what the wreck is wanted for — and that is a question the instruments cannot answer. The dark keeps the wood. It keeps it from us, too.
— Elias Frisk

sapwood = the outer, still-living rings of a tree, the last to form before it is felled

questions.prompt: What is this text mainly about?

options.text: That the cold, nearly fresh, and oxygen-poor Baltic preserves sunken wooden ships far more completely than warmer seas do.
That tree-ring analysis has traced the Sabina's hull timbers to oak forests on the far southern shore of the Baltic.
That leaving a Baltic wreck in the deep preserves its timber better over time, though only a raised wreck can be studied.
That improved laboratory conservation methods have at last made it safe to raise waterlogged oak timbers from the seabed without decay.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-004 (main idea / whole-text gist), edge position 1. WHY-KEY (C): the arc runs phenomenon (the Baltic 'keeps its shipwrecks the way few seas do', framing the 'haul the timbers up ... or leave them' choice) -> evidence (the split trial of twenty frames) -> complication (the reburied frames outlast the raised, treated ones) -> verdict, paragraph 5: 'It does not show that raising a wreck is a mistake; it shows that raising one is a bargain, in which the price is a slow chemical unmaking that the seabed spares you', set against 'A ship left in the mud is a ship no visitor will ever stand before and no student will ever measure by hand.' C paraphrases that two-sided verdict without lifting any distinctive four-word run. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = scope_error -- promotes the opening phenomenon ('The Baltic keeps its shipwrecks the way few seas do') to the thesis; tempting because it is the passage's first and most vivid image, but the text's argument is the raise-or-leave trade, not preservation as such. B = detail-as-main / wrong_location -- accurately reports the paragraph-2 dating finding ('pointed not to Gotland but to forests on the southern shore') but that is a sub-point, never the whole-text focus; tempting because it is a concrete, memorable fact. D = surface_word_match / outside_knowledge -- recycles the 'conservation bath' vocabulary into a claim the passage flatly refutes, since the raised timbers suffered 'a slow chemical unmaking'; tempting to a reader who anchors on the laboratory-treatment thread and misses the reversal.

questions.prompt: What does the text say about Renlund's dating of the ship's timbers?

options.text: The main hull planking was felled around the winter of 1627, from oak that grew on the southern Baltic shore.
Every one of the cored timbers still carried the intact sapwood needed to pin its exact felling year down with full certainty.
The rings were matched against a master chronology built from oak that had grown on Gotland itself.
The dating established that the ship had been repaired several times in the years before it finally sank.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. WHY-KEY (A): paragraph 2 states the wood 'had been felled in a narrow window around the winter of 1627, and its ring signature pointed not to Gotland but to forests on the southern shore', and that 'the felling year holds firmly for the main hull planking.' A paraphrases both the date and the southern-shore origin, keeping the planking scope. WHY-DISTRACTORS: B = quantifier_upgrade (the engineered dominant trap) -- the passage says 'The outer, datable sapwood survived on only a handful of the cored timbers', so 'Every one of the cored timbers ... with full certainty' absolutises a hedge the text explicitly limits; tempting because it otherwise echoes the real dating vocabulary. C = wrong_location -- inverts the origin: the chronology was 'built from Baltic oak' and the timber traced to the 'southern shore', not to 'Gotland itself'; tempting because Gotland is the salient place name in the passage. D = surface_word_match / too_far -- the passage only hedges that the lighter timbers 'may have come from later repairs' and calls that evidence 'consistent, not settled', so a definite 'established that the ship had been repaired several times' overstates a hedge; tempting because 'repairs' does appear in the text.

questions.prompt: According to the text, how did the two sets of frames compare during the first few years after they were divided?

options.text: The raised frames had already cracked apart completely along their ring lines within the first year out of the water.
The raised, treated frames looked like the clear success, while the reburied ones showed nothing anyone could observe.
The reburied frames were plainly better preserved than the raised ones from the very start of the trial.
Both sets of frames were kept entirely hidden from public view throughout those first few years.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order; the stem names the comparison but does not entail its direction -- the passage could have reported the reburied frames faring better early, so the answer is a reported empirical outcome. WHY-KEY (B): paragraph 3 states 'For the first few years the comparison seemed almost cruelly one-sided. The raised, treated frames were clean, catalogued, and on their way to a display case; the reburied ones were invisible, muddy, and doing nothing anyone could photograph.' B paraphrases that early one-sidedness. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = wrong_location / time-shift with a quantifier upgrade -- the cracking ('splitting some frames along the ring lines') belongs to paragraph 4's later drying, not 'within the first year', and 'cracked apart completely' overshoots 'some frames'; tempting because the cracking image is vivid and genuinely in the text. C = polarity_mirror -- flips the early result to its opposite pole; the reburied frames only prevailed over the longer record ('Then the longer record came in, and it ran the other way'), not 'from the very start'; tempting to a reader who remembers the ending and back-dates it. D = scope_error / over-broad -- the reburied frames were indeed unseen, but the raised ones were 'catalogued, and on their way to a display case', so 'Both ... entirely hidden' overgeneralises to both sets; tempting because half of it is true.

questions.prompt: What does the text suggest kept the reburied frames from decaying?

options.text: The polyethylene glycol bath had by then replaced all the water in their cells and fully stabilised the wood.
Cold, low-oxygen mud is guaranteed to halt the decay of buried wood indefinitely, whatever its history.
They had already shed the sulphur compounds that turn to acid once waterlogged timber meets the air.
Returning them to cold, airless mud restored the very conditions that had preserved the wreck for centuries.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-002 (inference / implication), one logical inch beyond the text. WHY-KEY (D): paragraph 4 reports that 'The reburied frames, sealed again in cold and airless mud, simply held', and that 'the very conditions that had preserved the ship for four hundred years went on preserving it.' D infers the mechanism -- the seabed conditions, restored, did the preserving -- without overstating it. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = wrong_location / cross-set swap -- the polyethylene glycol bath was the treatment given to the raised frames, the set that decayed, not the reburied ones; tempting because the bath is the passage's one named conservation method. B = quantifier_upgrade -- inflates the observed 'almost no change' into the absolute 'guaranteed ... indefinitely, whatever its history', a certainty the four-year record never claims; tempting because the mud clearly did protect the wood. C = outside_knowledge / too_far -- the passage traces the acid to sulphur compounds meeting 'oxygen and moisture in the gallery air', so the reburied frames were spared by the absence of air, not by having 'shed the sulphur compounds', which the text never says; tempting to a reader who supplies a plausible chemical reason.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude towards Oldenburg's rule that every wreck should be left on the seabed?

options.text: Convinced: the writer plainly shares her certainty that lifting any wreck at all is always a serious mistake.
Qualified: the writer grants the sea preserves best, yet finds that raising a wreck can be a defensible bargain.
Dismissive: the writer treats the case for leaving wrecks submerged as mere sentiment with little real evidence behind it.
Detached: the writer sets out both courses and declines to judge one against the other at all.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-005 (stance / tone / attitude), edge position 5; signature trap is attribution swap. WHY-KEY (B): Oldenburg states the absolute rule -- 'Leave them down there — always, every wreck, without exception' -- but the writer answers in paragraph 5 that 'The record from the Sabina is more divided than her rule allows', granting that 'The sea is the only conservator that never sends a bill' while insisting 'It does not show that raising a wreck is a mistake; it shows that raising one is a bargain', whose worth 'depends on what the wreck is wanted for.' 'Qualified' names exactly this real-but-bounded position. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = role_or_attribution_swap (the signature TYPE-005 trap) -- assigns to the writer Oldenburg's quoted absolute; tempting because her 'always, every wreck, without exception' line is the most emphatic stance in the passage. C = polarity overshoot -- pushes the writer's reservation into outright dismissal, which is contradicted by the concession that the seabed 'went on preserving' the timber 'unspoiled'; tempting to a reader who reads the raise-side case as the writer's own verdict. D = tone_misread -- 'declines to judge ... at all' misses the evaluative verdict that raising is 'a bargain' with a real price; tempting because the middle paragraphs weigh both sides evenly before the verdict lands.

fictional_entities: the Sabina, a 17th-century merchantman wreck off Gotland (invented)
Tobias Renlund, dendrochronologist (invented; all findings invented)
Marit Oldenburg, senior conservator (invented; all quotes invented)
generator_meta.question_geometry: Q1 main idea (edge, pos1) / Q2 detail / Q3 detail-comparison (STEM-LAW: stem names the comparison, answer is a reported empirical outcome) / Q4 inference / Q5 stance (edge, pos5)
generator_meta.key_letters: C, A, B, D, B — all four letters used, no monotonic run and no positional/alternation tell; keys mix specific-confident (Q2, Q3, Q4) with qualified (Q1, Q5) so 'pick the hedged answer' does not score blind
generator_meta.engineered_traps: quantifier_upgrade planted at Q2/B (sapwood 'only a handful' -> 'Every one ... full certainty'), Q3/A ('some frames' -> 'cracked apart completely'), Q4/B ('almost no change' -> 'guaranteed ... indefinitely'); attribution_swap planted at Q5/A (Oldenburg's absolute assigned to the writer) and cross-set swap at Q4/A (the raised-frame treatment offered as the reburied-frame mechanism)
generator_meta.schema_note: Emits 5 questions per the long_passage_5q blueprint (ELF long passages are invariantly 5q); this intentionally exceeds candidate-item.schema.json questions.maxItems=4, which was authored for single-question seeded eval items. All other schema constraints pass as-is.
